

Shawn (00:00:00):
Welcome everybody. This is the Q&A call for the Traffic Engine. It is May 6, 2021. I’m stunned that nobody asked any questions about iOS 14.5. I thought that was [crosstalk 00:00:12] going to be an entire topic.

André (00:00:13):
Yeah, I figured it would be the dominant one, yeah.

Shawn (00:00:16):
That’s what I thought too. It’s kind of funny to think about. Nobody asked but I’ll still share a couple of thoughts on iOS 14.5 just sort of a starting point. And these are just a few things to clarify if you’re doing anything in Facebook and Traffic Engine and you heard mention of it and you’re wondering what in the world, what’s really going on here? So, here’s the challenge. About 80% of Facebook’s traffic is mobile and 60% of that mobile traffic is on the iPhone. So, Apple has recently released essentially a prompt. And in that prompt you have to now opt in to having apps share your tracking data. In the past the iPhone allowed Facebook to use a phone number lookup system and that was done in the background by default.

Shawn (00:01:10):
And now it’s not. Now people are having to opt in and there’s some estimate that maybe half of the people who get that prompt will say no. I think it’s closer to round it up to a number, like a 100% of the people are going to say, no. I mean just as soon as I saw the prompt, I was like I would never say yes to this. So, what that means at the end of the day is about 20 to 50% of your Facebook performance data isn’t going to be reported accurately. That’s the bottom line. So, now we have this. There’s three things that have been going on. So, what did we think was going to happen, what actually happened when this was released about, I guess it was last Monday, and then what are the ramifications?

Shawn (00:01:53):
And we still don’t really know the ramifications. And there’s still some dispute about what has actually happened. So, just mentioning this now, just to orient us all to this that the sky is kind of falling in many ways with Facebook. Things are going to evolve. And really I also want to clarify, all this means is people are opting out of being tracked on the iPhone. They’re not opting out of seeing ads. It’s like a critic. I’ve heard people freaking out like, “What’s going to happen when nobody sees my ads on the iPhone.” That’s not what this is about. This is about their performance being tracked. So, I think I’m going to do a, I may do it as like a missing chapter or I may come at it as some other way to… So, there’s a broader understanding of what it means.

Shawn (00:02:40):
We will probably just integrate it into the content for the Traffic Engine as well. We’re still at that phase where we’re going to have a month or two before we actually see what’s really going on here. And we know some things that have happened. Facebook has changed the attribution windows and they’ve done other things that are very significant. For example, we can’t retarget opted out users. We can’t use our data for lookalike audiences. This is very significant, but we still need to see how it all plays out before we start making some sweeping recommendations. All right, let’s jump into questions. Let me get this out of my way. All right. So, first question that was asked, I’ll just read out. in general, I’ll read the questions as written. Sometimes I’ll summarize them. It’ll make sense why I do that.

Shawn (00:03:31):
And then once the question’s asked I’ll initially throw out an answer and then we can get Loren and André contributing as well. From the really hard questions I’ll just have Loren and on André answer. That’s my strategy. All right, here’s the question. As part of writing my own TDB durable business manifesto, I re-read your paid traffic manifesto. Simple Truth number seven, struck a chord with me as I’m struggling to understand why I’m getting good levels of engagement, my peeps, but not so much conversion or rather not as much as I wish I had. My question is how do you identify the weakest link in your system? How do you troubleshoot a business system? I’m so curious to have your perspective on this. So, just to refresh everybody’s memory, because I know that you’re sitting there with your copies of paid traffic engine manifesto, of course, a Simple Truth number seven, there’s only one way to improve your results.

Shawn (00:04:20):
I’ll just read parts of the story quickly, it’s short. When we accept that our businesses are systems, we realize that they’re subject to the insights of systems theory. Perhaps the most profound insight is this one from Eliyahu Goldratt’s theory of constraints. The only one way to improve the throughput of a system is to improve the performance of its weakest link. Now throughput just so we’re all clear and I should have defined that better. But when I wrote the manifesto, throughput is the rate at which your system produces its primary output. So, happy customers is the way we measure throughput, the André and I measure throughput. So, the rate at which we produce happy customers is our throughput. All right, in Goldratt’s terminology, we need to focus on improving the system optimum, not improving local optima. Improve the whole, don’t take [inaudible 00:05:01] with the parts layman’s term.

Shawn (00:05:02):
That means there’s always one and only one place to focus our attention to improve our results. We just need to know where to look and then act accordingly. Okay. So, what does that mean? So, we have to take a step back to answer this question. The first thing we need to be very clear about is what are you optimizing for? Because we need to know how are we going to define throughput and we need to do that with clarity. And so, that could be something as simple as I’m optimizing for sales, okay. That’s clear, and we need that level of clarity. So, and then once we have that level of clarity, we need to then take the next step and say, “Okay, what is our current reality? What is the rate at which we’re producing sales?” And that could be in a week that could be in a month.

Shawn (00:05:51):
That could be a 90 days, whatever it is. But we need to understand what the system is doing right now. And oftentimes it’s helpful to compare what the system is doing right now to some point in the past. When I ran an agency, one of the things that I would often do is compare whatever unit of a period of time I was looking at for now with a client to the same period of time last year or the previous several years, because that’ll eliminate seasonality and lots of other factors. If you don’t have that data, or if that data isn’t meaningful for any variety of reasons like you made a complete overhaul to your business, then you might need to compare say this week to last week. But if you haven’t really changed anything, then your ad spend is the same.

Shawn (00:06:42):
The system is the same. Then comparing one week to the next may not be as valuable as comparing, say two to four weeks. You’re trying to get a sense of [inaudible 00:06:55], what’s happening in this slice of time. I wouldn’t go too far because at some point, time, seasonality and other factors do affect the data. And that leads to another question, which is what data do you have? If you’re just sitting there and you have a whiteboard and you’re like, “Okay, I’m going to kind of understand current reality [inaudible 00:07:15] the whiteboard just has I spent X number of dollars. I got X number of clicks as reported by the media platform, which could be wildly inflated. Then I got X number of leads and I got X number of sales like that.”

Shawn (00:07:34):
If that’s the data you have, that’s the data you have. Then you need to get it for me, at least I need to get it all out where I can see it. And I want to put the data in some sort of order that makes sense. Now it sounds like what I’m doing is a linear analysis, which we rail about all the time and that’s not true. What I’m doing is I’m just seeing what’s happening. Because the next thing that I have to ask myself is, do I really understand this data? And that means a lot of different things. So, for example, I’ve seen this happen more times than I can count, unfortunately, which is somebody runs an ad for video views and they see the first piece of data in Facebook.

Shawn (00:08:16):
And it says you had, and let’s say it’s a seven minute video that goes to a landing page where they can opt in. And they look at the Facebook data and it says 1500 video views. And then they look at their landing page data and it says 10 leads. And they immediately do the quick calculation. They think that’s never going to work. What they don’t realize is that, they don’t realize how Facebook defines a video view and that what they really want to know is, how many people see all of the video. I’ve seen this happen over and over again, that people assume the data means something that it doesn’t mean. They assume in this example, the data means that 1500 people watched their entire video, whereas what it probably means is 1500 people of them, 1,450 had autoplay on.

Shawn (00:09:08):
And the video started going. And only what they really want to look at is what was the 50% view, what was a 95% view? That’s what I would really care about, like who actually watched this video? And then what percentage of them went onto the landing page to become leads. So, you have to really dig in. And this sounds like I’m almost avoiding the question, like how do you find the weakest link? But you have to find the real story that your data is telling.

Shawn (00:09:32):
And this is the part that gets overlooked over and over and over again, which is there is, it’s like, well, here’s the data and it’s in a spreadsheet. Who cares about that? What I care about is what is the data telling me? What story could I tell another person about this data that’s true, that the data tells me that confirms? Or are there things that the data disconfirms and you have to get that understanding first. I like to get all of that out of my head onto something, because I’m very visual. André and I are both very visual. So, I could get that out of my head so I can see…

Shawn (00:10:13):
And I think about I stand back and look at it. I literally stand back and look at it, put it on a whiteboard. I need to be able to walk around. Sometimes I stare at it for a while and I leave it. And I’m trying to think about what could happen, but from that, ideas begin to emerge. The first thing that’s likely to happen is what you think is true about what’s going on is not at all true. So, that’s the first thing we need is precision and clarity. What’s actually happening? How many people, let’s assume it’s a text-based ad and that ad then goes to a landing page and that landing page has, let’s say a 1,000 words of content and then an opt-in. Okay. And then there are X number of emails and an opportunity to convert at the end.

Shawn (00:10:57):
And that whole thing takes 14 days or so. We need a period of time where we have enough data in that system to understand what actually happened. And from that, we’re going to see some things emerge. One thing that might emerge, because generally problems generally come in. In my experience, they generally come in two buckets. Bucket number one is traffic, bucket number two is conversion. Those are the two big buckets. So, that’s where I start looking. So, traffic problems show up as, well I got a lot of… And there’s not always a clear delineation, but if you have an ad that say really curiosity driven, and you’re getting tons of clicks on the ad, but nobody [inaudible 00:11:44]… And then you look at the, the bounce rate for your landing page, or the time spent on your landing page is really low.

Shawn (00:11:50):
And then you notice that you’re resolving the curiosity early, and we see this happen a lot and the ad opens up some bit of curiosity, and then it gets resolved in with the first paragraph. So, people scratch their itch. And what that shows up as then is like very low, a short time on site, lots of clicks. So, that’s telling you a story. They’re like, “Oh, okay, I’m getting a lot of curiosity clicks. I’ve answered the curiosity. Nobody needs to go. Nobody needs to move forward.” This shows up in e-commerce too. I had a client years ago where one of their golf retailer and one of the companies that they sold didn’t allow their prices to be displayed on a website. They had to be displayed in the [inaudible 00:12:36], dumbest thing in the world. Absolute dumbest thing in the world.

Shawn (00:12:39):
So, what would happen is it looked like their cart abandonment was way higher than it actually was because people were just price checking, right? So, you need to know the story your data is telling, right? So, once you know that story, then this is where in general, you’re going to find something that, and it is probably the data is not going to point to it. In a linear analysis, the assumption is the data will tell you. It’s like, okay, you’ve got a 1,000 clicks and then a 100 people opted in and of those 100, 10 purchased. And you’re like, okay. So, if I could get another 1,000 people and that’s a linear analysis. And what a systems analysis says instead is like, what’s going on here in total? And what do I think? this is the hardest word.

Shawn (00:13:34):
This is the most difficult concept people want to hear when it comes to an analysis like, just think. You have to step back and you have to think about what could be causing this. And then that’s where you form a hypothesis and then ask yourself, is this defensible? Did I look at the data that I have and make the case that this is true? And is there any data that I have that could disconfirm that this is true? It really is that complicated. And I don’t want to suggest that there’s a sort of magic. When you read Eliyahu Goldratt’s book, start with The Goal. It’s a novel, but it’s incredibly valuable, but you’ll realize that there’s work in finding the constraint and the constraint moves. Once you address a constraint well, it shifts someplace else.

Shawn (00:14:28):
So, the big constraints tend to be, the traffic source isn’t that great. Another constraint tends to be that you’re providing the wrong incentives in your ad, that you’re opening so many loops and you’re generating so much curiosity in the ad that somebody wants to click on it to find out what’s next. That’s often a very big constraint. Another constraint is that you’re making a promise of lots of free goodies. And those free goodies are so attractive that somebody is willing to just give a throw away email address or their email address and unsubscribed because they want the goodies, but they don’t really care about the offer that follows or you’ve generated just enough curiosity for the goodies that you’re offering, but they don’t really need anything after that. You didn’t really get their sustained attention. What you got was a sort of a sugary attention where it’s like, “Oh, that report looks good.

Shawn (00:15:23):
I could use that. That answers a question of save it on my desktop with all the other things I’ve downloaded and never read like that.” That’s another place to look for it. Or there’s also the lack of congruence so that you have, instead of having a single thematic arc, where your ad relates to your landing page relates to you, how you build relationships, relates to how you sell that it’s disjointed. And when it’s disjointed, that’s actually the constraint. It’s an underlying constraint. It’s a conceptual constraint. It’s not one that you see in the data. It’s just one that shows up where nothing seems to work well together. And the reason it doesn’t work well together is because it’s not a whole thing. It’s a bunch of parts. And this is really how, and I don’t want to get on my soap box and start railing here.

Shawn (00:16:08):
It’s like 8:20 in the morning and I’ll get super animated before I’ve had enough coffee. But this is the thing that pisses me off to no end, is that what people are selling here comes a soap box. Now I can tell my heart rate is increasing. What people are selling online so much is bullshit. It’s this idea, if you do this part really well, then the whole thing’s going to get better. And that’s just not true. Especially now the best example is lead gen. It is really easy to generate cheap leads, and you can find an infinite number of systems to generate and courses that how to generate cheap leads. But no businesses aren’t built on cheap leads. Businesses are built on customers. So, the things that you do to get cheap leads often undermine everything that follows like big promises, a lots of lead magnet stuff like pile on the bonuses for signing up.

Shawn (00:17:01):
And you do all of that and you completely undermine everything that follows. And then people scratch their head and wonder, “Why don’t my emails convert?” Because you’ve got people in with the wrong incentives. So, making a consistent incentive structure, which is, I have something valuable to share with people who have these desires, these needs, these problems to solve. And I’m not going to bribe you to do that. The broad, the underlying value is the fact that I’ve identified for you this issue that you might have, and I’m going to show you how you can solve it. That’s it. If someone responds to that, well, that’s excellent. But if it’s, if you pile on 5,000 bonuses to get them to hand over the email, you’re pushing, rather than letting the value that you’ve created pull. So, that’s a very long answer.

Shawn (00:17:50):
The underlying message is there is no formula for this. You have to be so intimately familiar with your data and the story that the data is telling that when you do something, you sense what happens in the data after, and it’s either what you expected or what you didn’t. That’s how you confirm and disconfirm. And it’s very much a process of saying like, “I understand what going on in my business. I understand how the parts all work together. I’m going to do this thing. And if this thing makes a positive difference, here’s how I think it’ll show up. If it doesn’t, if it makes a negative or neutral difference, then this is what won’t happen. And then I’ll sort of know that thing didn’t really contribute much or didn’t contribute at all. Or I’ve taken a step backwards accidentally.” Then you have to explain to yourself how could that have happened? That’s, I’m going to shut up now. André, thoughts on this one. You’re a systems guy too.

André (00:18:47):
Yeah. I think I’ve got a few thoughts. You’ve said most of them. So, I’ll just zoom out slightly and I’ll see if I can mixed analyze my thinking. I’ll just think out loud for a second year. So, one of the things you mentioned is we look at all of this through the lens of systems thinking and systems thinking there’s an idea called synthesis, which is really the opposite of analysis. So, analysis is taking something complex and breaking it down into this tiny little part and then looking at this part in oscillation of the system whereas synthesis is about understanding the whole and the part at the same time and the relationships and connections that make up the dynamics of the whole. So, they’re very different. So, with that idea set to the side, there’s another idea, which is when you’re looking at anything in oscillation, you don’t have, there’re certain things that you don’t have, which gives context.

André (00:20:02):
So, context requires more than one thing. It’s looking at something and comparing it to something else. So, to get contrast, you need more than one. So, you need… Shawn mentioned that you can compare data to other points in time. So, I’m just talking more hypothetically here, more big picture. So, what you could do is instead of just driving traffic and then getting this thing that’s happening. And I think the part of the question, I don’t remember exactly what it said, but it said something to the fact there was engagement, but there’s no sales or there’s very little sales, something like that.

André (00:20:54):
So, I think there are many people that will engage with stuff with content. And I don’t know what the context around engagement meant. Maybe people clicking between pages or opting in, I’m not sure what the engagement context was. But there’s many people that broadly speaking are tire-kickers or freebie seekers, and they were quite heavily engaged with content all day long. They’re on all the warrior forums, they’re [inaudible 00:21:24] and chatting away. But none of them actually doing anything in the real world, they’re not making much money but they love engaging. So, engagement, sometimes you need to look at it in context. So, I think I would go all the way upstream to the highest point, which is the traffic source and do something completely different. Let’s just hypothetically presume that maybe the traffic source is faulty or need some tweaking.

André (00:21:57):
So, I’d do something completely different, try and find a slightly different pool of people perhaps, or the ad that you put in front of those people is completely different. So, therefore it draws the attention of a slightly different segment of people within the group of people that have been targeted and then do the same thing. And now you’ve got two pieces or three pieces of stuff to look at. And suddenly now you’ve got context, and you can get a bit more meaning from the data, as opposed to just looking at the data in isolation and scratching your head and wondering why. Well there seems to be engagement here, but there’s no sales, which doesn’t say very much other than there’s engagement and no sales. So, that’s the end of my proof.

Shawn (00:22:47):
One thing I’ll add too, just before I turn it over to Loren, for his thoughts on this. The place… Oh, I said one thing, I’m going to say two things. So, first when you’re trying to find the thing that makes the system change fairly dramatically, you’re looking for constraints. Don’t test little stuff, right? We think about AB testing, this headline versus this. Don’t do that. If you’re trying to make a system intervention, especially early, when you just don’t know what’s working and what isn’t, make big changes because big changes tend to show up. And the best big change, like the highest leverage change in my opinion, like this is the secret to all internet marketing I think. We’ll just give it away on the call at all whatever eight of us there are. The biggest leverage is in the expression of the offer.

Shawn (00:23:42):
The offer is an expression of a point in time or some combination of factors with a product. It’s not a product or service, it’s the expression of the product and service and some sort of context. That’s where all the leverage is. And if you think about it, and I can’t remember where, this is not my example of somebody else’s. I prefer to credit others when they come up with these ideas but I can’t remember this one, but if we were opening up a restaurant and we said, “What do we need to have a really great restaurant?” Someone might say, “We need a great menu. We need on and on and on.” But what we really need is a hungry crowd. I think it was Mark Joiner who used that example.

Shawn (00:24:19):
I think two products ahead I think. If your offer is… I remember I went to Woodstock in 1993 in the huge music festival and I paid a lot of money for food and for drinks that I didn’t really like because there were no other choices and I was there for three days, right? So, it was a hungry crowd. The product, because it was, there was literally a hungry crowd, the product kind of sold itself. So, one of the places to really focus in this, and from my experience working with 220+ clients over the years, the almost all of the leverage is in the offer and how it’s expressed and how it matches up with an audience’s needs. When you get that right, everything’s easy. When you get that wrong, nothing is easy.

Shawn (00:25:14):
And it’s like, that’s where all the leverage is. So, if you have a system, that’s just, for whatever reason, it’s just not producing the results that you want, look at the offer first and ask yourself, “Am I talking about things that are keeping my prospects up at night? Is this something that would be fine to have, or what I’m giving them is kind of enough because it’s not that big of a desire? Or am I really speaking to these deep visceral core emotional needs?” Because once you do that and you make that connection, lightning strikes, and then you can’t miss it in the data because everything just comes together. So, that’s I think probably better advice is, start by looking at your offer. All right. Loren, anything to add to this conversation?

Loren (00:25:59):
Yeah, I think the way I think about it is I look at the journey that someone goes through as they’re engaging with your ad and your offer. And I think that to tactically implement what Shawn and André, what you guys were talking about, looking at the story the data tells, what I think is helpful the way I think about it is, okay, take a Facebook ad for instance. You have your number of impressions. Well, that’s how many people have seen the ad. Then you have, you can take a look at your frequency. Then you think of, okay, your CTR, that’s how many people have had any click on the ad. Then you take a look at your CTR, your link click-through.` Well that’s how many people have actually clicked over to your landing page. You can take a look at your landing page views.

Loren (00:26:45):
You can take a look at clicking over to say, add to cart, right? Whatever the next step is. And let that kind of map that out. Then what you can do is compare that to a baseline from what would be expected. And that takes a little experience or talking to someone who has experience about, well, here is the CTR that we would be expecting for something like this. Here’s the amount of leads we’d be able to the landing page conversion and all the different conversion points. And that may be helpful to identify how yours is different. Now it’s important to point out that that does not necessarily mean, okay, my CTR is lower than people are telling me, well, I need to do whatever I can to boost my CTR.

Loren (00:27:36):
That’s exactly the opposite of what Shawn and André, were talking about in terms of looking at it as a system. However, it does help you understand the story that the data is telling when you can look and you can see how my interactions differ from what is the industry standard. That’s when you begin to understand for me, at least that’s when you begin to understand the story that the data is telling. When I can see, yeah, my-

Loren (00:28:00):
Understand the story that the data is telling. When I can see, “My CTR is lower than many people would expect it to be, but my landing page conversion is so much higher, and my quality of lead or my quality of customer is so much higher.” Well that is because… The story my data is telling me is that I’m sacrificing CTR, I’m getting fewer clicks, but those clicks are way more qualified, and that’s because of the strategic decisions I’ve made to qualify before that click.

Loren (00:28:32):
That’s how you begin to understand the story that the data is telling and that’s how you begin to implement what Shawn and André… What you guys have talked about, in terms of letting that story then dictate what your next steps are.

Shawn (00:28:44):
Great stuff. That’s a really great example of how to think about that. All right, let’s move on to the next question. This question, I’m going to paraphrase a little bit because this is one of the most informative questions from an audience perspective, the value that’s able to be extracted from it and I don’t want anyone to get caught up in the details.

Shawn (00:29:10):
So, Mike, this is your question. I’m not going to read the question as it was written. Instead, I’m going to paraphrase. The question is… my dog gets involved here too. For everyone who was on the very beginning, I have my dogs here, at any point going to start barking insanely loud especially when I start talking.

Shawn (00:29:31):
All right, so I’ll read parts of this. I reviewed modules three and four plus related Q and A call and this week, set up an ad group. This is a Google ad group with a single keyword phrase match with negatives geography, bid adjustments as instructed, $50 per day budget. My initial goal for paid traffic is 10 clicks per day. This is an offer that he has sold offline, so he knows the offer as attractive. So that significant price point project fee of $5,000 and he’s trying to sell this offer online with a strategy call approach. So the campaign has been live for less than 48 hours. There’s no actual hard data to work with. In the meantime, I’ve learned enough about Pay-Per-Click to have serious doubts that the campaign is intellectually defensible based on the traffic engine framework. Here are the few signals he’s picked up already.

Shawn (00:30:24):
There is little, if any active intense search, the perfect search for his service doesn’t serve up ads relevant to what he’s offering. Let’s see, he’s done the work. Mike congrats, this is outstanding. This is a textbook example of how to do the work. Okay, based on the above, I’m making an educated guess and I’m trying to convert low quality traffic. Finding a responsive Facebook audience is probably a more intellectually defensible paid traffic strategy. Am I thinking logically about this? Okay. So this is the question. This is why Facebook exists. So, back in the day, forever ago, when we just had ad-words of the GDN, it was difficult to find there were, if this is what and it actually, let me take a bigger step back. This is where when I created the traffic triangle, I actually created that, or the traffic pyramid.

Shawn (00:31:26):
I created that to be on the flight in an answer to a question like this at an event I was speaking, and someone was trying to figure out like, where’s all the traffic. And so I came up with these three categories of traffic, the people who are looking for you specifically, and you could be you as a person, or you as if you have a name system. So if somebody searches for Andre by name or searches for auto responder, madness by name, that’s a very high quality pool of traffic. And it’s very, it’s a low volume, relatively low volume. That’s the tip of the pyramid, the next layer below that people who are looking for what you have to offer. So that could be… That’s where things get broader. It could, it’s how people might look for you without knowing about you.

Shawn (00:32:15):
So they might say email them, learn email marketing. If they’re looking for auto-responder magnets or a story-based email marketing or whatever it is. They’re not looking for Andre specifically, or for the course specifically, but they’re looking for the… They’re actively looking or searching for what that course offers. That’s the second pool, generally higher quality, or it’s still high quality traffic, very competitive, not often, not enormous amounts of quantity. Then there’s this last pool. And this last pool I’ve talked about is that these are people who, if they knew you or what you had to offer exist might be interested. And that sounds like it’s sort of something I made up on the fly, but it’s the most precise example I can give for the problem that Mike is facing.

Shawn (00:33:13):
And it’s a problem, many of us face, which is there are people out there who, if they just knew this value that I’ve created existed, they’d want it, but they’re not looking for it. They’re not searching. Now. Sometimes that means they’re not actually interested in it, but a lot of times it means they don’t even know what to look for. They don’t even know that that help exists. And that’s the, in the past that the Google display network was really the only way that we could approximate that traffic, but because of banner blindness and other things, it was very difficult. So the way we would do it in the past is we might find an article that was about a certain subject. And that website allowed Google display network advertising. So then you think, oh, somebody is reading this article and they see an ad for my stuff, which is, I think is it would appeal to someone who would be interested in this article.

Shawn (00:34:06):
Like that was the weirdness that we used to do. And it worked in a backward traffic was cheap. Then Facebook came along and Facebook is the system that finds that enormous pool of potential traffic at the bottom. And what generally happens now that Facebook is so popular is that no one goes through this process that Mike went through, which is really the way the traffic engine is laid out. That yes, absolutely start with active intent with search, because it’s the quality of someone who is actively looking for you is almost always going to, if you look for every hundred prospects, the people who are actively looking are going to be far more valuable than the people who are sort of pap, passively, bumped into it. That’s we can just all agree that makes sense, but what happens is when people often hear like, oh, I need to do Facebook advertising.

Shawn (00:35:01):
So they’d go right to Facebook and they skip the part to find out, are there actually people looking for this already, Mike did the work and realized that it doesn’t. The thing that he’s offering is very specialized. And it’s unlikely that there’s going to be enough traffic there to sustain a business, because they’re just the people just aren’t looking for it, probably because they don’t know that it exists or the way that they might look for it for help. Like they don’t, they’re not even sure how to look for help. That’s a very common in many markets like these. They know there’s a problem, but they don’t even know how to think about who might be able to help them solve the problem. So that then suggest that Mike has in all of us who were in similar situations, that’s where we turn to Facebook because we can, what Facebook allows us to do is to think about the factors that might contribute to somebody being a perspective or perspective client.

Shawn (00:36:06):
So it could be job title. In this example, it could be interests in groups around certain topics might be an indication. It could be lots of different things. This particular market that Mike is working in as relates to veterinarians. So you could find groups that where people who own vet clinics belong to, you could, there are lots of ways you can do that, but that makes a lot more sense after he’s realized that audience is not looking for his offer, the mistake that could have been made. And it’s why the traffic engine is written the way that it’s written is that you skip over your right to Facebook. And you know, this is where these are my people I’ll just show them ads, but you don’t realize that every day, they’re 50 100, 200 people looking specifically for what you have to offer, and that’s the low hanging fruit.

Shawn (00:36:54):
So the way Mike has approached this is absolutely textbook. And it’s exactly the right way to do it. And now he has, because he’s done that he has some data to work with, and that data suggests that he should move on to Facebook, but I wouldn’t turn the campaigns off. I would let them run. I think it will be low traffic. It might be low traffic, but when it does hit, it might be ultra high quality. It might produce a customer every few months or every month, who knows. I wouldn’t turn it off, but I would immediately move to Facebook because Facebook is going to reach that enormous part of the audience. That’s just not actively looking, but once they know it exists, they might start nodding their head up and down. And I’ve looked at the site, Mike, and it’s excellent.

Shawn (00:37:43):
I like everything that you’ve done here is excellent. I think now it, you have all the resources in place. You’ve checked the box that you’ve gone through Google ads. I would leave those things running. And then I would immediately go to Facebook and I would target and reach out to Loren for any questions about that, on how to target that audience. But I would then take what you’ve created. The value proposition that is very clear. You’ve converted a client already. And I would start using that on Facebook. The lesson here that there’s sort of, one of the underlying lessons is don’t go to Facebook first in general, you find the search traffic, but also when the data tells you something, listen, and the data has told Mike that this is that there just isn’t a lot of search traffic there, right? Like don’t… what people often do then is they get frustrated.

Shawn (00:38:38):
Like I’ve done all this work. So I need it. It’s like this, it’s, it’s sort of this idea that you’re winning. If you played poker that you’re pot committed, like I’ve put a bunch of money in the pot. Like, what are you still have a bad hand, right? That money’s gone. So Mike has put the money in the pot, but he got a result. And that result is there’s not a lot of traffic to be found here. So respect that result and move on to the next thing. That’s most likely to produce a result, which in this case, I think for this particular offer and this particular audience is going to be Facebook. And, and that’s the void that Facebook fills. So congrats to Mike for doing this exactly right. You’ve got all the infrastructure in place now I’ll move on and do Facebook next or Andre. This is probably not a lot to add here, but on a few either, if you want to add anything to this.

André (00:39:25):
No, I think you covered it really well.

Shawn (00:39:32):
Andre, are you sleeping now dreaming about it?

André (00:39:34):
No, I don’t have anything to add. You’ve nailed it for sure.

Shawn (00:39:44):
Let’s move on to the next question. This is actually not a traffic question, but it’s a fun question. So we’ll do it anyway. I hope it’s valuable for everybody. All right.

Shawn (00:39:58):
Hi, Shawn and Andre. I really appreciate the opportunity to do a Q and A. I have an SOS, the sphere of influence style site that leads to an autoresponder madness style email flow, 20 emails in total, really heavy on the teaching. My lead magnet is five mistakes. You have to avoid when getting solar to your house. So it sounds better than Swedish. My goal is to get them to sign up for a quote from one of my solar installers. Basically I’m doing paper lead for still solar installers, but I can’t figure out is this.

Shawn (00:40:25):
I’m getting a lot of unsubscribes over the email flow about 50% of total subscribers when I’m also getting a shit load of love in my inbox, that would be a great band name. Shitload of love. How should I drill down to further understand how I should evolve my email flows? Okay. So I will give a short take on this, and this is really Andrea’s area of expertise. I didn’t have to be convinced to serve a mistake, a decision that we made. So a couple of things to think about. So it’s 20 emails. It’s heavy on the teaching.

Shawn (00:41:37):
This is interesting. I don’t know. If you’re using 20 emails or trying to convince somebody to do solar. And I don’t know if you’re convincing somebody to make that decision, I’m not sure. I’m not sure the timeframe on that is 20 emails. Right. I know for my own example, this, this is something I thought about years ago. Didn’t really do much with it. Thought about it again. I believe it was two years ago, had somebody come out, do the site visit everything else, then decided not to do it then, but it was always kind of in the back of my mind, then this spring decided that this the time was right. Shawn (00:42:41):
So the assumption that someone’s going to be ready to buy. And a lot of it also, what you haven’t mentioned here is how you’re getting the traffic. You’re going to a lot of unsubscribes, 15%. And what you described as a shitload of love in your inbox. Good. Right? Like you don’t, you’re never going to make everybody happy and if you’re not polarizing at all, then I try to be in that middle ground.

Shawn (00:43:43):
And I think the fact that you’re getting a lot of people who are bailing out and raising their hand and saying, I’m not right for you or this isn’t right for me. However you want to think about it, right. That should happen. But in the fact like if you’re getting unsubscribes and then nothing in the hearing crickets, then I would be nervous and like, oh, I feel like I’m doing something wrong, but unsubscribes is not, it’s not necessarily a negative. We have plenty of people on our email sense. Every time we send out an email, we get a big jump and unsubscribes, right? That’s the game. It’s just not right for them at that moment. But the fact that you’re getting a lot of positive feedback that tells me that you’re just, for whatever reason, you’re a little polarizing and polarizing is good. André (00:44:42):

So I guess one idea would be to be very polarizing and try and weed out people before they opt in. André (00:46:09):
So then people that are opting in are the exact people as best he can make out that are people that are almost ready or ready to get a quote they’re in that, in that stage, that phase where they’re going to be shopping about to figure out how much this is going to cost and which supplier they’re going to go for.
