I’m not sure.

Shawn (00:47:11):
Yeah. I don’t think it’s paid. I think he’s sowing weeds. You just said something that I don’t know that we’ve ever articulated. This is always fun. I learn more on these Q and A calls. Anybody else does? There’s an interesting, a couple of, this gets back to the first question. This is a perfect example of systemic effects. Like we had a question asked earlier, another question asked later the two don’t seem related in any ways yet they point to this emergent idea that now I hope I can articulate is I really set myself up. So when we’re looking at something holistically in a systems level, you can think about a couple of, and Andre just said this a couple of different ways that we can approach solving, quote, unquote, a problem. Right? So if we have lots of unsubscribes, we’re kind of making an implicit decision, which is where we’re making the doorway to the lead a little wider, knowing that we’re going to lose people over the email series, or we could narrow the door more at the beginning, and we would expect to see fewer people unsubscribe over time.

Shawn (00:48:22):
Like that’s a systemic emergent property. That it’s a really good example on how to think about like, think about your business from a systems view. But that requires that you take this step back a little bit and say, okay, I’m getting a lot of leads, but those leads are not converting. Or we’ll just use this example. I’m getting a of leads, but over the course of a really long email 20 plus emails, very long campaign, I lose 15%. Well, okay. Am I losing, like now it’s a matter of like trying to figure out the story here. Am I losing 15% because I’ve made some, I’ve opened the door in the beginning wider so that people who are probably looking for things like how much does this cost and they’re looking for information. That I’m attractive to them, but they’re going to opt out by the end.

Shawn (00:49:20):
Like that’s a possibility, let me say, okay, well, how would I test that? Well, one way you could test it is you could, you could narrow the entry point. I mean, you can sort of qualify the traffic a little more. And what would you expect to see happen if you did that is you would have fewer people unsubscribe, if you narrow the door even more, or if you narrow the entry point and you still get the same number of unsubscribes, well, that’s puzzling, that’s not what you expected to happen. So then, then you start asking your self questions like, well, how, how could those two things be true? And that’s really the game, right? It’s not, it’s not really the data as much as the data. And it’s not just it’s, it’s the data and yes, it’s the story, but you, you kind of have to know what you think is happening and why, even if you’re wildly wrong there’s, there’s a story I just happened to reread it last night, Michael Lewis’s new book.

Shawn (00:50:18):
So it’s, it’s fresh on my mind, but there’s, and I don’t, I have no idea if this story is true, or if it’s apocryphal, it doesn’t really matter. But the way, the way this I’ve heard this story a million times, and it’s actually connected to this question because I believe the soldiers were Swiss, but a group of Swiss soldiers in the outs, but gets there in a snow storm, horribly lost. The one of them pulls out a map and they follow the map back to camp and they get out of the blizzard, everything else. And when they get back to camp, they realized that they, and they learn the Alps, but they realize that the map that they have was from the Pyrenees, I completely wrong map, but just having a map, gave them the confidence to find their way home, or it’s completely lucky.

Shawn (00:50:58):
Right? So like sometimes just having a narrative gets you started, even if the narrative is completely wrong, right? I mean, if in this example, if you just say, okay, here’s my narrative. I think what’s going on here is I am attracting people who are tire kickers, who just have they’re really early in their research phase of solar. And I answered the questions that they have in that early stage that many of them realize that solar’s not for them. And they just opt out and, and that’s okay. Like, that’s what I think is happening. Well, that at least as a position that at least as some way to make sense of the world, then when you do things and you make interventions and you change things, you can continue to compare it against understanding of the world. And at some point, I mean, if you’re right, it will continue to be consistent and you’ll see, you’ll do things and you’ll get a result that you expect, but more often than not what will happen.

Shawn (00:51:56):
And this is the basis of the scientific method. What will happen is you’ll, you’ll see a result that does not make sense based on what you did and what you understand of reality. Like the now your narrative does it match. And science is just a continuing attempt to get us, to have our narrative more closely match reality. So then your, then your narrative has to change a little bit. You think, well, all right, this new piece of information suggests to me that my understanding wasn’t entirely accurate and that’s fine. Like it’s not what you think, oh, I was wrong. You know that, no, it’s not that it’s your understanding of reality now is a little more refined and a little, and it matches. And that’s excellent. All right, Lauren, anything you want to throw in here? I realize this is sort of not here really touched on your area of expertise, but certainly stuff you’ve been doing a lot.

Loren (00:52:54):
No, actually I thought you covered it really well. I could probably, wax on just to talk, but no, I think I think that was pretty good.

Shawn (00:53:06):
Wow. It’s philosophically, right? One more of the initial questions then we can, there are a couple of others I think, sort of randomly around. All right. Last question. I just realized I had one other more straightforward question. Driving Google ads to a four step multi-step sphere of influence inspire multi page pre-sell it’s a mouthful that’s converting at around 5%. How big is the potential to increase the volume of leads when adding re-marketing? What should one aim for 50% increase a hundred percent. This is a great question. Like what do we think the impact on lead gen is of layering on target? The answer is, of course it depends. The honest answer is who knows, but let’s actually unpack this question a little bit. So we know a certain number of people. Well, he’s using Google ads, so there’s active intent. So he’s driving people who are actively looking for something to a four step process, four step front end.

Shawn (00:54:15):
So immediately I have a little bit of concern about that because, and what I would want to do immediately before I thought about retargeting at all, is I would want to drive that traffic to a one-page version and see what happened to conversion only because there’s active intent. Someone’s looking, somebody wants something right now. Now it depends too on what they’re looking for. If they’re looking for information and you’re using a front end, that sort of gets them the information they want sort of in a step-by-step ways, but not like a quiz funnel, but similar idea, then that’s kind of grew it where, where I see some things get wacky with traffic as when, and this is why I don’t like creating. I don’t, I generally don’t like starting with Facebook traffic because Facebook traffic is great for a longer front end. and so then you, and you take that.

Shawn (00:55:07):
And then when it’s time to go to search traffic with active intent, you have to like shrink that way down for a lot of the market because they want something right now. They don’t want the example I use the traffic into, I think is if your roof is leaking, you don’t want to download the seven steps to hire the right roofing contractor, right? You want something to show up and that day and fix your roof. So that’s why generally simpler with Google for highly focused search terms. And then as you get less than, as you get farther away from the bulls-eye of his first term, you get more of the multi-step process and that more closely matches Facebook. So, that’s sort of a first stop. Then the question, this question comes down to when do, what do we expect from retargeting? So here are a few different scenarios. If you drive traffic, highly focused search traffic,

Shawn (00:56:01):
[inaudible 00:56:01] traffic, highly focused search traffic to a multi-step front end. And then what I would re-target… Well, and then you’re going to have to wonder what kind of impression did you give somebody. If I wanted something right now and you put a bunch of barriers in my way and you re-target me, I might be irritated. So, that might depress results. What I would do in this situation I think, is if the Google traffic is highly focused, I would send them to a shorter front end. If that’s not an option for some reason, I would re-target to a shorter entry point that really… Again, this gets back to, I’m trying to make a hypothesis here. Why am I seeing the results that I’m seeing? So my hypothesis might be that I’m putting too much in front of somebody who was actively looking for a solution right now, how do I figure that out?

Shawn (00:56:58):
Well, I could send them to a shorter version and see if that converted differently. I could re-target them to a shorter version and see if that… But that to me seems that seems one step removed. I’m dancing around the advice I want to give very clearly, which is if you’re driving targeted Google ad traffic to a multi-step front end, the first thing I would test before retargeting is a driving that same traffic to a scaled down version of the front end, with fewer steps straight through, get them, and then look at conversion with that intervention. I would do that first because my suspicion is the active search intent is a strong signal that these people want something faster, maybe not based… And this is the, the solar example from before. So maybe that’s not true in this particular thing. But I know like for me, if I want to quote for, If I’m sitting at home and I’m thinking solar seems interesting to me, I would really like, you know, how do I get quotes for solar?

Shawn (00:58:09):
What do I need to do? I get to a landing page and search for solar installation quotes, and I get to a landing page and someone is extolling the benefits of solar, why I should be interested in solar, I’m already interested. I told you that when my search intent, I’m way farther ahead. The search term needs to match where I am in that way multi-step process. If the first three steps are getting me interested in solar, and then the fourth step is where I learned that I can sign up to get installation quotes, and I’m searching for installation quotes, you’re just pissing me off in those first three pages. I’m already there=, I’ve told you what I want to do, let me do it.

Shawn (00:59:02):
Now I don’t know that’s true for this example, but that’s really a way to think about this. The reason this applies to the question about adding retargeting is the reason you would add retargeting is you can make the case that there’s some percentage of people who are still high quality, who didn’t take the next step forward because of some reason that you can affect positively, right? So that’s a mouthful, but it could be something like Andre and I are writers. We love the written word. We have a long ad that sends people to a lot of content on a landing page, and then it’s time to opt in. And they’re like, eh, you know, maybe not, you’re not quite there. One thing we could do, we wouldn’t do this because of, and I’ll explain why we wouldn’t do this.

Shawn (00:59:55):
But one thing we could do is we can think, well, we’ve actually had some feedback from our last workshop. A lot of people seem to like the video screen cast overview thing. So maybe there’s a percentage of our audience, potential audience, out there that would respond better to video. So the hypothesis would be that by changing the medium from written text to video, we might attract that 15, 20% on whatever it is of our potential audience that responds more to video. So then we would re-target people, new leads who stayed a certain amount of time, but didn’t convert. And we would re-target them and then have sort of the equivalent messaging that we had in our written work, in a video. I mean, we’re having a conversation about things. And then we would expect that maybe some percentage of those people would respond to that.

Shawn (01:00:48):
Now, the reason we don’t do that is we don’t really offer a lot of video content. It’s rare in our courses. So we don’t want to attract people, but we don’t want to put out the wrong bait and say, okay, come on in. Here’s a video. We know we now know that you prefer video and oh, by the way, if we ever sell you anything, there’s a very little video in it. That’s just in-congruent, that’s a systemic consideration. We’re looking at our whole business system and saying, yes, we could find a way to improve the local Optima. We could get an extra, say 15, 20% of our leads. We could recapture by showing them video but we know at the system level we’re eventually going to make those people unhappy. And we’re optimizing for happy customers and we’re not going to make them happy because we brought them in for the video, knowing we don’t do video.

Shawn (01:01:43):
That’s a really great example of making a systemic consideration that we could boost at the local level and probably undermine our results at the overall system level. I feel like I’m dancing around the retargeting but what I want you to hear is how I think about this question because there is no actual answer to this question. It entirely depends on the source of traffic, how you’re presenting the information, the where in the process that traffic is in their own mind, or are they ready to sign on the dotted line for an installer? They just want to compare prices? That’s all expressed in search intent, my advice is answer all of those questions first. Then ask yourself, are you driving that traffic to the right step based on their search intent?

Shawn (01:02:38):
This is why it’s very important to make sure that when you’re structuring your campaign, your Google ads campaign, that you think about intent and that you’re matching intent as expressed in the search term to the appropriate step in a multi-step process. So that if someone is saying to you that they’re interested in solar, they’re just sort of interested in solar in general. The benefits of solar, or whatever it is, that search would work really well with a longer education focused front end that then gets the lead. But if someone is saying that they’re telling you they’re way farther along, that’s why we break up our ad groups really focused on intent. All right. That was a lot. Loren we’ll start with you, and then André can be the heavy hitter on this one.

Loren (01:03:30):
Yeah. Retargeting a lot of times it’s not necessarily that people have said no, a lot of times they’ve just for whatever reason said not now. We like to think as marketers, that people see our Google ad or our Facebook ad and the world stops and they consume our content and they sit there and they think deeply about it and consider what we’re having to say. But in reality, they may be checking their phone at a red light. They may be checking their phone for 10 seconds before they rush off to do something else. We live in a busy, distracted world, and the reality is sometimes we may be a perfect fit for that person and they may be interested, but for whatever reason, they got distracted. A lot of times retargeting is just the chance to get in front of them again at a time when maybe they aren’t as distracted.

Loren (01:04:22):
Retargeting is really simple. The way I think about it is where do people fall off, and then you get in front of them again, and then you consider the economics because we are talking about paid traffic. So retargeting ads do cost to put in front of those people. And can we do that economically? And in a way that that makes sense for happy customers? That’s the type of thing you can’t really have a definitive answer of this’ll work, this won’t work, or it’ll two exit, it’ll five exit. You really just have to put the feelers out there and see what happens.

Shawn (01:04:56):
Yeah, that’s great nuance. I want to draw attention to what Loren just said in a slightly different way. I’m thinking about this as a 30,000 foot view. What does it mean based on behavior and other things, but there is this other fundamental truth, which is, I know for me, I was thinking about it being wine at the grocery store. I don’t have Facebook on my phone I have in the past, but what I imagined is that you’ve got that five to seven minutes, you’re standing in the line in the grocery store, and you’re looking down at your phone and something looks interesting and you click on the ad and you read it. Often times you may click on the landing page. And it’s interesting, you’re reading it.

Shawn (01:05:38):
It’s definitely interesting, but in that moment, it’s really entertainment is what’s happening. Even if it’s super interesting, the likelihood that by the time you get out into your vehicle or your home or whatever, you’ve kind of forgotten about it. It’s actually an act of service when the reminder shows up in Facebook again later. I’ve had this happen to me, any number of times where I was like, oh I actually appreciate it. Like, oh, that thing showed up again, that I clicked on before. It can be irritating sometimes if I wasn’t interested. There are plenty of times where I was looking at the way and the nuance of it. What Loren did was mention the obvious part. Get a few bites at the apple for those people who just, it’s not that they don’t care about you or what you have to offer in this moment.

Shawn (01:06:30):
It’s just when they looked at it before, maybe they had five minutes on their phone at the grocery store, or they were at work and they checked Facebook quickly and they started to engage on the phone. Whatever it is, there is some percentage, for each market it’s different, but there is some percentage of people who, as Loren said, it’s not “no”, it’s “not now”.

Shawn (01:06:51):
That’s where internet marketers ruin things. We have this sort of running commentary in my head about how internet marketers ruin everything eventually. Where internet marketers ruin this is where it becomes insistent. It feels insistent. It’s like, hey, you looked at my stuff, so here’s my stuff again. Hey, it was three days ago you looked at my stuff and you haven’t looked at my stuff again, here’s the stuff. Or my favorite, I look at something, I actually buy it, and I see ads for it again. There’s plenty of ways to do it wrong. Let’s not forget to do the obvious and doing it right. Which is just take a shot at showing people the same thing a few times and see what happens. Andre, I know retargeting is your favorite topic in the world.

André (01:07:42):
Ah no, not really other than running one test of anything is never the correct answer. I wouldn’t have thought just 5% sounds great. Maybe it’s 10%, or maybe 5% is awesome, but without running any other versions, who knows? Maybe the multi-step is shown to desktop only, and maybe the single page, the long form single page is for mobile only. Maybe the mobile gets its version of its best version. Maybe that’s 8%, but it’s not 8% if you run it for both. Maybe the multi-step is a similar dynamic, you remove mobile from it, and it goes from five to six or seven. So then you’ve got these two versions now that are well optimized and then obviously layer on the re-targeting, which was the point of the question.

Shawn (01:08:49):
That’s such an important point. This is part of what it means to be in business, you’re just updating your narrative all the time. Like, oh, if I do this, this happens. The one thing to think about with Facebook, I think this is somewhere in the traffic engine, as it sort of came to me as a revelation as said Zen parable, “You can never step in the same stream twice”. That’s less true for Google ads. When someone is expressing, the same search intent, there’s sort of like a daily inventory of people looking for the same things. I think, yes, there’s certainly context affects that, but in general I think that’s more of a constant. I think that’s a little more reliable. So when you A/B test in Google ads with highly focused search intent, I think the A/B testing is fairly reliable.

Shawn (01:09:42):
I don’t think that’s true with Facebook, Loren and I have had some really in-depth conversations about this and, he has convinced me, and I’ve continued to convince myself that Facebook is a stream that you step in that is never the same. Context is so important. Think about if you’re an American and you were a Trump supporter, what was your state of mind the Saturday after the elections in the United States, when Joe Biden was declared the winner of the election and the Facebook ads you saw that day. If you were an ardent Trump supporter the Facebook ads you saw that day probably landed with you differently than one that you saw two weeks earlier, two weeks later. Same is true if you were a Biden supporter, right? We had different emotions, but that was an emotional day in our lives as Americans.

Shawn (01:10:42):
So depending on which side you were on, we had different emotions, but if I were doing an A/B test and it included the period of time after the U.S. Election, and I tried to compare that to the same period of time the year before, that’s just not an accurate test. Context matters so much in Facebook, because Facebook is engineered context. People believe their newsfeed represents the news and it’s anything but that, it’s a confirmation feed. Your news feed is a collection of things that Facebook thinks you’re likely to respond to based on past behavior. That’s what your news feed actually is. So if you in the United States, if you click on a lot of Fox news articles, you’re going to have a very different view of the world than if you click on a lot of Huffington post articles. One is a more right-leaning news source the other is a left leaning news source. Your feed’s going to change, your reality changes.

Shawn (01:11:45):
So I think from a testing perspective, it’s particularly hard to say this versus that. And in Facebook, especially, I don’t know, that was sort of a random thought. All right, there are a couple of questions in the Q and A section, actually. One comment. Thanks Mike. And then let’s see… So there’s the first one, “What have you heard about server-side tracking and using that? I keep hearing it’s super complicated to get set up, but also that it is crucial”. I am going to have a special guest interview on here, [inaudible 01:12:20] I haven’t even mentioned this to André yet. I won’t reveal, eh should I reveal? I’ll reveal. Scott Desgrosseilliers, founder of wicked Reports, is a really good friend of mine, really wonderful human being, and just on many levels is a sort of a Renaissance guy on a lot of different topics. I asked Scott if he would be willing to just come on just for an interview for the traffic engine audience, to be very clear, we have no financial relationship with Wicked Reports. I’m not going to suggest Wicked Reports as a tool.

Shawn (01:12:50):
I’m going to explain what it does well, but this is not a sales thing. This is not a “why is Wicked Reports the greatest thing since sliced bread?”, It is a, now that the world has fundamentally changed, and it’s going to change a lot more because of Apple’s privacy updates, Google’s doing the same thing. Privacy is going to be much more center stage, tracking is going to be much less accurate. Scott, is a data scientist at his heart, he’s not a marketer. He’s the first to say that, but he is able to articulate, tracking and attribution in ways that are so fascinating. He’s just a fascinating person. So I’m going to put together probably five questions that I think are most valuable to traffic engine audience. Then Scott and I are just going to record a conversation. I’m just going to let him riff on those questions.

Shawn (01:13:43):
I haven’t figured out the questions yet, but I have a pretty good idea about some of them. I don’t know an exact date for that, it’ll probably be in the next few weeks. I want to have some of the iOS 14.5 stuff, play out a little bit. There’s still some unknowns. There are still things we just don’t know how they’re going to play out, and his perspective will be really interesting. That’s a long way of answering the question by saying, I’m going to find somebody smarter to answer that question instead. All right. I think there was one other… Robin had posted in chat. He said he “started sending paid traffic to my new multi page pre-sell last Friday. What are your thoughts on the result?” Okay, so I looked at this a moment ago, so this is what I would expect to see.

Shawn (01:14:34):
I suggest everybody to click on the charts, so they can see it all. I’ll kind of give you the highlights here. So this is a four-step process, four pages, 1.8, was it 1.8000? So 1800, 1,810, it looks. People see the first page and then 556 have made it to the last page. So around 30%, a little less than 30%. I want to say five out of four people are bad at math. So, 30% ish make it all the way through. That’s the first thing I care about. That’s the first inflection point, right? It’s how many started? How many ended? That’s the big picture. A third, to me, I like that. I look at that and I think, okay, I’d want to know the next piece of data though, of those people who see that. Here’s the data that’s missing that I would want to see to have a better understanding, which is after that fourth page, I assume there’s an opt-in.

Shawn (01:15:31):
I would want to see the rate at which people opted in, because the pieces of information I want are, how many people start? How many people become leads? How many people see the last page? How many people see the last page and then become leads? And then the other crucial point is how many people go from first page to second page. Half, 54% went from the first page to the second page. That tells me, I see that as that you’re doing a good job, frankly. Now some people might look at that and think, oh my, you know, 44% of them, 46% of the people didn’t make it past the first page. Well, people click on stuff all the time and bail. So, the fact that half the people who are seeing your first page, and then it doesn’t drop off precipitously after that.

Shawn (01:16:20):
Right? It’s kind of a gentle curve you see in the, in the middle, there’s that almost a little bit of a belly there that I like to see. Which is, you can’t see my hands that I’m doing. What I mean is the middle is kind of flatter, right? It’s not like a set of stairs where it’s very dramatic each step. I like to see the middle, a little flatter where you’re pulling them through the meat of it a little bit here. So, what can you do with this data? How would we use it? We could form some hypotheses. So here’s one hypothesis that I have, which is that in general, this is doing well. It’s doing what it’s supposed to. You’re going to lose most people’s attention. If it’s not a bleeding neck, dire need for something that’s just reality.

Shawn (01:17:13):
People are entertaining themselves. And you’ve provided half of them, a little squirt of dopamine and a little bit of entertainment, and that’s fine, then they’ve bailed. They should, most people aren’t going to be interested and you’re seeing that in the data. So, that doesn’t bother me. The question then becomes what do we do with this data? My answer, which is probably going to sound ridiculous, but I wouldn’t change a thing right now. I would keep sending more traffic. This traffic seems to be doing what you want it to do, but I’m only seeing part of the data. I don’t know at what point did these people have the opportunity to become customers? What is the rate at which they do that? It’s… Robbins, sorry, he just put some other information.

Shawn (01:18:02):
So the page three is the lead capture. So this is generating 30% overall leads? Okay. So, that’s excellent again. Well, it’s excellent, out of context. You have no idea what those leads are doing, the quality of those leads and what they’re supposed to do. So the context matters more than anything else. Robin I don’t know if you want to throw this in chat or not, but what happens after this? They’ve come in and become leads, at what point do they have opportunities to buy and are they buying anything or do you not know yet? What I’m seeing right here, this looks great. I would just want to know, okay… Seven day soap opera sequence, any purchases yet Robin? Watching the chat here, and then they get put through his purchase funnel after the seven day. So, we have incomplete information is the short answer, the larger observation here is that this looks good from a traffic perspective, right?

Shawn (01:19:14):
That’s important to draw attention to, because somebody who doesn’t really understand paid traffic might look at this and say, “oh wow, you have this huge opportunity to move more people from page one to page two”, is that true? Well, maybe. Somebody else might look at this and say, wow, this is outstanding. You’ve managed to eliminate 46% of your audience in that first step. Okay. You’re getting rid of the tire kickers. You’re still getting, the overall result is still outstanding from a lead gen perspective. Robin mentioned he really polarizes on page one and page two, excellent. Right. Also good. Here’s another example of how easy it is to game the system for no positive effect. I could come in and convince Robin, maybe I could convince Robin that I’m super smart and I can help his business grow.

Shawn (01:20:19):
The problem I can help him solve is I can get, let’s say I can recapture 25%. We’ll do easier math. It would be 21%, that I look at this and I think you’ve got a huge opportunity here. I’m going to get that 54% page to conversion rate up to 75%. Write me a big check and we’re going to make it happen. That is such an easy thing to game, we can rewrite page one, page two, where we open up a bunch of loops and we create a lot of curiosity that has to be resolved and we can push people to page two and make it look like we’ve made a big improvement. Like, oh, 75 is bigger than 54. Yay! You know, bigger, better, excellent. Right? And have no meaningful impact on the rest of what happens after we wouldn’t affect throughput at all.

Shawn (01:21:14):
We might even make throughput worse. There there’s so many variations of the way of how to do this. So what Robin needs to do now, is have some patience. Which is hard, but I wouldn’t stop doing this, yet. I don’t know if this was like a traunch of traffic and you stopped, or if you’re continuing for this to run, but I don’t see anything here that tells me, this is like sugary sweet traffic. That’s that you’re just pulling through. Especially if you’re polarizing on the first page. I would leave this, but ultimately what matters is what is happening at the purchase side. He’s paying for traffic, we pay for traffic with an expectation of a financial return greater than what we paid to acquire the traffic in some timeframe. Now that could be if it’s a short window to just recoup your ad costs, and then there are long-term backend is where you make your money, which is typical for many businesses.

Shawn (01:22:18):
Then that’s different but, Robin needs to ask himself, at what point should he be seeing some data about the financial performance of this of this process? And then, he knows if it’s happening. Let’s just for the sake of argument, say this, and this looks like it’s 30 days of data. So ,30 days we still don’t know though what percentage of these people are, where they are in the process. So that’s again, a missing piece of data. How many people have seen email seven? How many people are in the purchase funnel? That’s the data that Robin needs to be keeping track of, and this is the challenge when you send people into a longer sales process is that you have to be more patient, and you have to then rely on looking at this data for some nuance to feel comfortable continuing to pay for the traffic.

Shawn (01:23:25):
Things I would be looking for as well Robin, are you getting emails back from people there? Do you create opportunities for them to interact with you? Are they? How many are unsubscribing? How many are opening? If there are clicks, how many are clicking? Not because you want to optimize any of those variables, but because you want to have a holistic picture of what’s happening. This just tells a little slice of it. This slice that I see, I like the story that it’s telling so far. It’s telling me that you’re weeding out people early, but you’re not losing a lot of them after. So that that’s the front page traffic cop. I like.

Shawn (01:24:01):
The front page traffic cop, I like, so this doesn’t…I would continue spending money on this, if it were my account. That’s a long way of saying it. Loren? André? Anything to add to this one? I don’t know if you guys want to weigh in on the sort of esoteric topic here.

André (01:24:19):
Yeah. I’m thinking this is looking, my gut feeling on what’s in front of me here in this image, it’s looking really, really good. There’s a few ways to look at this, so 30% of all traffic ends up on the email list and that seems pretty good because there’s a lot happening before somebody opts in. Now if you look at page three, of the people that land on page three, 74% of them, so it’s a 74% opt-in rate of the people that land on page three, end up on the email list. Well, it’s because if he’s done all of that filtering on the front to be able to get a 74% opt-in rate. So, that suggests to me that those leads are very qualified. Yeah. And I like that, I mean, you could never get a 74% opt-in rate on page one without getting the worst leads in the world onto an email list.

André (01:25:34):
But now you’re getting 30% of all the leads onto the email list, which is good because you’re doing all the filtering upfront yet 74% of the people that land on page three, what they’ve seen on those first three pages, including the ad, so that’s another step. So there’s an ad, there’s three pages, on the third page is an opt-in and they’ve gone through this and 74% of them have opted in. That seems pretty good to me. I would run that alt. I would keep running that and I think again, just thinking out loud, without knowing any sales data at this point, any optimization that I would do, I would probably do on the email end. I would play around with the different things, with your relationship sequence, with the promotion sequence, doing a hybrid of both, but it looks like this front end, so the sort of front end piece that’s creating these leads for you, these prospects, I think looks good. Really good. That’s all I’ve got.

Shawn (01:26:46):
Anything to add?

Loren (01:26:49):
Yeah, no, I think it looks pretty standard textbook, it looks great! A lot of people see that big drop off after page one and they start to kind of flip out, but I think that’s quite, and it is particularly if you’re polarizing on page one. So I think you’ve done a great job.

Shawn (01:27:05):
And it’s just so important to think about context for traffic too, in Facebook in particular, I mean, when do you step back and realize that 80% of Facebook traffic is mobile and I don’t know how your campaigns are set up, so you can keep that in consideration too, but that’s really the environment that we’re in and age is a factor too. There’s so many things, depending on your audience, that all of that goes into the narrative, what’s actually happening here? It’s so easy to look at a chart like this and just see numbers, like there was 1,810 here, 556 there and then to make some assessment, but the context in which that exists, which partly it’s your targeting, it’s your offer, it’s your audience. It’s all of those things that from all of those little data points emerges something that no one of them tells you, that’s really a foundational part of systems theory is that none of the points matter, it’s what emerges, it’s the relationships that our interactions and what emerges from them at a point in time. Those emergent properties, so we can look at this and say, “Oh, okay, well, this is a tiny slice of data.” But when we start layering on the texture and the richness of who are these people? What devices are they on? Can we imagine… It’s different for me to imagine my mother on a mobile device looking at Facebook than my mother on a desktop device using Facebook. I can sense the context shift there, or imagining my mother on Facebook versus my wife on Facebook. That’s different targeting of course, but there’s so much nuance that goes into this understanding of what you’re doing, and that’s so important. And the beauty of what we’re able to do more, when we’re very close to the data, like Robin is close to his data, Robin is bringing that… That’s really the frame through which he’s looking at the data, we do that intuitively when it’s our data. We start thinking about who are these people, because he knows how he targeted, he knows sort of what’s happening, what the content is, and that creates so much context and nuance.

Shawn (01:29:38):
And then it’s just a, and this is a fun exercise, I did this with clients forever, so I’ve practiced it a lot, but it’s a fun exercise to do for yourself, which is: write the story you think your data is telling, like you’re explaining it to somebody else. And write it as a narrative, and not like, “Oh, Joe did such and such, but it’s…” Okay, well, here are the main data points, but then you have to shift to… It’s like, “Here are the main data points, here’s the context, here’s what it means” and what you’ll find in that exercise, and actually write it, and I would sort of share my framework, my method, but it’s so inelegant and so ridiculous and probably so useful only to me.

Shawn (01:30:24):
But I go on a treasure hunt, when I used to do this with clients, I would go on a treasure hunt and I loved finding data. So I would go and I would find data and I would just scratch it down on pieces of paper or whatever, but I would see something that was interesting to me and then sometimes I, maybe it might be a screenshot, but I would assemble all of that and then try to figure out what does this mean? And when I would sit down and write the narrative, you’re writing the narrative, literally typing it out to another person. So for me, it was typing it out to explain it to a client, often to recommend a course of action. So I had to make my case like, “This is what I think is happening. This is why I think it’s happening and because of that, here’s what I think we should do.” What you quickly realize when you do that is if you don’t have all the information, that becomes very clear very quickly, but then you’d go look for it. So if you think X is happening, but you realize the data isn’t really saying X, you go look for something that would confirm or dis confirm that X is happening, right?

Shawn (01:31:40):
So an example, this is not a great example, but it would be sort of the method that if Robin said to me, “I really want you to figure out for me, if this is working. I’ll give you access to whatever you need, and then let’s figure it out.” I would probably start with this, okay, I wouldn’t want to see the ads, I just want to see the targeting and kind of understand who these people are, and then I would understand their behavior over time, and then I would try to sit down and say, “Hey, Robin, here’s what I think is happening, here’s some of the high level view of what’s happening and here’s what I think it means that somewhere in that very likely”… And you just heard me doing it as I was asking Robin questions, but at some point in that I would realize, “Well, there are things that I don’t know and I need to know that before I can make an assessment or make any recommendations.” So then I have to go find out that information or find out that I don’t have that information and therefore, I can’t know that question without that information.

Shawn (01:32:40):
And it’s just becomes this, and this isn’t something you do every 90 days or every month, or frankly every week. But it’s something that you need to have as a habit of thought, a habit of work where you can at least periodically… Well, early on, you have to start laying the groundwork. So you begin to get an understanding of if you set up an ad, you know the targeting, you’re like, okay, great. And then you begin to understand the ad performance, what’s happening with the ad, what are the comments like? Is it being shared? Look at the stats, oh okay you get a sense of that and then what you’re looking for when things are wildly different over time. You kind of get a sense of what each of the things is doing and then it’s just a matter of contrast. But you do that for all the parts and those parts are creating kind of a dynamic understanding that you’re refining over and over, and then there’s the danger.

Shawn (01:33:45):
So the benefit is that you do that and you start seeing insights because you’re close to it, you understand how it’s working. The danger is you become blind to things, you become blind to your assumptions. In the intelligence community in the United States there’s something called red teaming. It’s definitely something that I recommend, if you’re working with clients, clients sometimes are okay with this, oftentimes don’t want to pay for this, but if you’re doing it with yourself, red teaming is I think very helpful.

Shawn (01:34:17):
And what red teaming means… So in the US intelligence community produces intelligence assessments, they’re not finite known things. I mean, we’re spying. We’re putting together human intelligence, signals intelligence, all kinds of forms of intelligence, satellite imagery, and we’re trying to make… The intelligence community, to the best of its ability, is trying to make an assessment like, “Hey, there are weapons of mass destruction in Iraq.” That was an intelligence community assessment. What a red team does is come in and try to tear apart that assessment, where can they find holes in it? Where’s the data? Where’s the conclusion not supported by the data? Where’s are the conclusions maybe not actually reflective of the data? Whatever it is, or where are big assumptions being made?

Shawn (01:35:04):
So red teaming your own narrative is helpful too and if you really want to have fun, and you have a friend who is in the game too and wants to deal, you can have somebody else help, but it’s actually much better if you learn to do it yourself, because what will happen, this is very much like writers who can edit as they write. What you can do over time, and when I say over time, this is six months, eight months, 12 months of habit in practice, but over time you red team yourself in real time. You’ll have an idea and you’ll immediately challenge the idea and when you challenge the idea, you’ll go look for evidence that really is strong or you realize you don’t have evidence that’s strong and because of that, you sort of put that idea into this maybe category like, “Oh, this could be happening.” And it just becomes a habit of thought.

Shawn (01:35:59):
So that wasn’t actually a question that was asked, but it’s something that I highly recommend because no one will ever understand your business as well as you do. That’s just a simple fact. I worked with, as I mentioned earlier, over the 21-ish years, it was over 220 clients. I never understood it very… Well… And I want to be cautious about how I say this, oftentimes I understood how their business was really functioning, from the perspective that I saw better than they did, only because they were blind to it. But what was also often true was that there were subtle nuances that only they knew that I didn’t know. The very subtle things that, and I mention that because you are the person who will best understand what your data is telling you, and it should become part of the fabric of your DNA, that you just know it. So when something happens that’s odd, that becomes really valuable to you.

Shawn (01:37:06):
You maybe create a new ad and for whatever reason that new ad you express the value proposition differently and you got a wildly different results. For most people, that’s not going to stand out, but when you’re sensitive to that system that is your business, those little blips really stand out and they can be acted on. And the only way to get there, frankly, it’s just practice. Or you just have to be in it doing the work, I don’t want to say every day, but you’re in your business essentially every day, every workday.

Shawn (01:37:40):
So, I don’t know, I’m sort of off on a tangent here, but any other questions before we wrap up? If we don’t have questions I’m going to do just sort of a lightning round at the end with André and Loren and I just to add anything that we think is important. No more questions? All right. So thank you everybody for coming today. I know for some of you, it’s very early, but let’s…Loren I’m going to put you in the hot seat first. So what is one thing of value you’d like to share with the audience? And you can choose, it can be anything you want it to be, and then I’ll have André share one and I’ll put myself in the hot seat as well.

Loren (01:38:21):
Oh, wow. Wow. Wow.

Shawn (01:38:22):
No pressure though, right?

Loren (01:38:23):
Yeah, yeah no, I think I would tell you this, I kind of was thinking about iOS 14 for the past couple of days. I actually was surprised that we didn’t have any questions about that, I kind of figured that would be the dominant topic, honestly, but so thinking about iOS 14, really, it appears that everything is going well from what I’ve observed and what I’ve kind of learned from other people in the industry. It was a shaky about two or three days in the beginning, but everything seems to have more or less calmed down.

Loren (01:39:00):
So for people predicting, I mean, we still don’t know what will happen, iOS 14 is rolling out in stages. I just got the prompts, I think yesterday and so many people don’t have it yet. So it will roll out over a course of weeks, the impact may take several months until it’s felt because the algorithm is receiving less information so it’s essentially getting stupider. So the impact of that we’ll continue to see, but I think it’s having less of an impact than people thought.

Loren (01:39:33):
One thing that is important to point out about iOS 14, and I don’t understand the technical specifications, maybe Sean, this is something you and Scott can talk about, but Facebook is still able to report on one event from someone who has opted out. I guess, if they could report on zero events, it would break so many apps. So they’re allowed to report one event. So what Facebook has done has set up this weird system, this new system of allowing advertisers to prioritize which events get reported. So long story short is some data is still leaking through to Facebook and because advertisers can prioritize what goes through, they’ll prioritize the most important. So the most important data is getting through to Facebook. So it’s actually, I wouldn’t say business as usual, but the impact has been not nearly as dire as many people were thinking. So that is a good sign and I guess I’ll leave it at that,

Shawn (01:40:41):
I appreciate you putting yourself out on a limb like that too. The rest of the world wants to say the sky is falling, you’ve heard it here first, Loren is more confident. [crosstalk 01:40:50] It takes courage. Well, you’re never going to be a good politician.

Loren (01:40:54):
That’s no, I’m too honest. I’m way too honest to be a politician.

Shawn (01:40:56):
Exactly, you’re far too honest to be a politician. All right André you’re up [crosstalk 01:40:59] Wait go ahead Loren, sorry finish…

Loren (01:41:03):
Oh, no. I was going to say that is an early kind of assessment, I’m not throwing down the gauntlet and saying that’s the gospel truth, but from what I can tell, it seems to be okay.

Shawn (01:41:12):
All right, André, any thoughts to share with the audience while we have their attention in far less time than we usually spend, these are usually three hour calls so I feel like we have a buffet of time to work with here.

André (01:41:28):
Yeah. I love the idea that you externalized just now about writing narratives around your data and you used to do this as a workshop or a thing with clients in the past. And I think when you said that it immediately reminded of one of my close friends, Mike Mindel, he’s the founder of Wordtracker, the old search engine tool from back in the day and it’s still going strong apparently, but we were in Vegas the one time and I had asked him to show me how he thinks about coding. So how he thinks about thinking like a programmer. So he showed me some of their back ends and I noticed that there was a whole bunch of stuff that was narrative driven.

André (01:42:26):
So although he’s coding, he would write narrative based user stories around various elements of what he wants the tool to do, how somebody interacts with the tool, when they interact with the tool, this thing needs to happen and it was all narrative driven. And I thought that was such a nice idea. And it’s something that I’ve never forgotten about how some programmers approach programming.

André (01:42:54):
So not that long ago was listening to AZ 16, one of their podcasts. One of the episodes was on the new Amazon book, or one of the new Amazon books called “Working Backwards” and they were talking about it on the podcast, I’ll put the link inside the chat window there. They were talking about how Amazon, how Jeff Bezos insists that PowerPoints are out. And so they replace these 20 something page PowerPoints, or these a hundred page PowerPoints with a six page narrative. That’s his law. That’s the only way that they do meetings. So the people that are working in the company have had to get really good at writing six page narratives, because the narratives are far richer.

André (01:43:40):
You can see and experience all the nuance that’s happening. Whereas just looking at raw data, everyone’s looking at the same data but everyone’s got their own narrative in their head and unless you, as the person who’s preparing this presentation in Amazon in this example, their the one that actually has all the context. So it’s their job to create this six page narrative, then everybody reads it. So whenever they go into a meeting in Amazon apparently, for the first 15 minutes of the meeting, or 10 minutes I forget how much it was, it’s just silence. Everyone’s reading the six page narrative. And then at some point everyone’s read it and suddenly conversation happens. And now everyone’s on the same page and everybody can start to talk around that narrative, that now makes clear sense to everybody in the same way.

André (01:44:40):
So yeah, that reminded me of their podcast. I picked up the book, but I’ve not read the section in the book about, I’m just looking at it now, but I haven’t read it yet. It’s put on page 83. It says, “How to write an effective six pager” and basically it’s their process of writing a six page narrative. This one is in the form of a PowerPoint, but at the end of the day, it’s about creating a narrative around data. So it’s completely relevant to all of us here. And it’s just a good reminder for me to read the section and actually start to do it myself, because it’s not something that I’ve done around data. In fact, I’m not very good at looking at data, which is a horrible admission, but I know Shawn loves it. So he’s got his head inside of wicked reports. That’s it.

Shawn (01:45:36):
I wasn’t… It’s funny, you just teed up a perfect, I’ll just, I’ll extend your thought a little bit and the context will make sense in a second. So I’m about 80% of the way through Michael Lewis’s new book called “Premonition” I love Michael Lewis as an author and “Premonition” it dropped Tuesday I think. I can’t put the damn book down, but “Premonition” is about the US, and in many ways, the global response to COVID-19 and frankly, it’s terrifying.

Shawn (01:46:10):
If you would’ve asked me what I thought happened with the U S response. I would’ve said, “Well I assume there’s some place in the federal government where we’ve been paying attention to pandemics for a long time. We have a plan and it’s the CDC and the National Institutes of Health…” And what Michael Lewis did is he found as he generally does, he just found really interesting characters, but the reality of the US response to COVID and really in many parts, the global response was that it was a very, very small group of people back in the mid 2000’s through chance, through George Bush reading a book about the 1918 influenza, and realizing we didn’t really have a pandemic response in the US government, which is frightening, and some very smart people from very disparate parts of the world. Many of them, not just government, came together, 77 total, a very small number, and then put together through… It’s such a fascinating story that it’s worth reading just for the story, but what, the part that what André was talking about, it’s amazing to me and the upshot here is that it was really fewer than 10 people essentially saved humanity from an infinitely worse fate.

Shawn (01:47:36):
One example in the book is that the conventional wisdom was that measures that we’ve been doing to slow the spread of COVID-19 like social distancing and closing schools and other things didn’t work. And that came from an analysis of the 1918 influenza that was flawed. We looked at one city, Philadelphia, and those interventions happened a week before the peak, it was too late. But when they did a retroactive analysis, a small group of very smart people on their own time did an analysis of the cities that weren’t hit as hard. What they found was that those social distancing and other similar interventions earlier reduced death rates by half or more. So an insight that nobody else saw, this small group of people saw, they published it in academic papers, other things.

Shawn (01:48:28):
But the part that André just said that I thought was fascinating is that there was an email chain, beginning as a small group, one of the members of that group happened to be someone incredibly intelligent who was looking at data coming out of China and translating data and looking at data that we had in the US early in the pandemic, when the federal government was saying, “This is not going to be a big deal.” And they as a group were projecting the what if scenarios, what if this actually…These factors are true? But all of that was done in narrative format, all of this high level data, the R0 of the virus, the transmission rates, the death rate, the estimated death rates, all of those things was a very small group of people who were having conversations, Zoom and others, but mostly it was driven by a narrative. That one person actually understood what was about to happen and was trying to raise the alarm and was using the narrative format to take the data and say…

Shawn (01:49:44):
And it had a metaphor that he used, which is there was a tragic accident, I don’t want to say accident, but a tragic situation in the US when firefighters were killed, the Mann Gulch fire, which was, I can’t remember where it was in Nevada or someplace, but it was firefighters parachuted in, they had a plan to fight a fire, when they got down there on the fire was on the other side of the river, and most of them died. And it’s an example of what happens when a fire increases speed and power exponentially. A fire that looks a long way away moving slowly as it gains exponential power it went from moving like 1.2 miles an hour to 40 miles an hour in a very short period of time. These are all the things that context and narrative and metaphor convey. And that is actually how we understand things. I highly recommend the book, just because everybody should read it to realize how close we were to an infinitely worse disaster and how a very few number of people essentially saved us as a country and in many ways saved the world from a far worse fate. If you think that you’re not, if you have this idea that you can’t make the difference, read that book, and you realize that a very small number of people and individuals make an enormous difference in the right situation. But it also, it’s just an extension of André’s thinking about the power of a narrative, the power of actually understanding what’s happening and putting it in some context and projecting out its implications. Like, if this is true, and this is true, and this is true, then this is likely to happen, that’s a super power, an absolute, super power. So that’ll be my closing thought for the day. Thanks as always to Loren for joining us on this excitement, it’s always, always a pleasure. Thanks to André, of course, for the opportunity to have a thinking out loud session. And thanks to all of you who are on the live call today for submitting your questions and joining us for the time.

André (01:51:55):
Right. Thanks everyone. Thanks Loren.

Loren (01:51:56):
Thank you.
